answer:
# Audit Planning Deliverables

---

# Engagement Strategy

| Dimension | Detail |
|---|---|
| Client Background | Not available in provided document/context — no uploaded financial statement or entity-specific information provided |
| Financial Statement Context | Not available in provided document/context |
| Engagement Scope | Audit of financial statements in accordance with ISA 300 (Planning an Audit of Financial Statements); scope to be defined by engagement characteristics, reporting objectives, and applicable financial reporting framework [S1] |
| Applicable Reporting/Audit Considerations | ISA 300, ISA 220 (Revised), ISA 210, ISA 315 (Revised 2019), ISA 330, ISA 230, ISA 260 (Revised), and ISA 240; ISA for LCE applicable if entity qualifies as a less complex entity [S4] |
| Key Audit Focus Areas | To be determined following risk assessment procedures under ISA 315 (Revised 2019); high-risk areas to receive experienced team members and greater resource allocation [S2] |
| Planned Audit Approach | Risk-based audit approach; overall audit strategy to be established first, followed by a detailed audit plan addressing nature, timing and extent of procedures at the assertion level [S1] |
| Preliminary Engagement Activities | Acceptance/continuance procedures per ISA 220 (Revised); independence and ethical compliance evaluation; agreement of engagement terms per ISA 210 [S1] |
| Initial Engagement Consideration | If initial audit, communication with predecessor auditor required; review of prior period financial statements and opening balances for material misstatements [S4] |

---

# Planning Memorandum

| Planning Element | Detail |
|---|---|
| Audit Objective | To plan and perform the audit so that it is conducted in an effective manner, enabling the auditor to express an opinion on the financial statements [S3] |
| Scope | Audit of the complete set of financial statements for the relevant reporting period; scope defined by engagement characteristics identified in the overall audit strategy [S1] |
| Reporting Framework | Not available in provided document/context — to be confirmed per ISA 210 engagement letter terms [S4] |
| Significant Account Areas | Not available in provided document/context — to be identified through risk assessment procedures under ISA 315 (Revised 2019) |
| Audit Approach | Risk-based; risk assessment procedures performed early; further audit procedures (tests of controls and substantive procedures) designed in response to assessed risks per ISA 330 [S1] |
| Timeline | Planning to commence at or shortly after completion of the prior audit engagement; interim and final audit stages to be scheduled; key cutoff dates to be identified [S1] |
| Management Responsibilities | Preparation of financial statements in accordance with the applicable financial reporting framework; management does not assume auditor responsibilities through planning discussions [S2] |
| Audit Team Responsibilities | Engagement partner to review and approve overall audit strategy and audit plan; key engagement team members to participate in planning and team discussions; direction, supervision and review to be documented per ISA 220 (Revised) [S1] |

---

# Materiality Calculation

> **Note:** No uploaded financial statement or entity-specific financial data has been provided. The materiality calculations below present the standard professional framework and benchmark ranges. Actual figures must be populated once the financial statements are made available.

| Benchmark | Source Value | Percentage Applied | Calculated Amount |
|---|---|---|---|
| Profit Before Tax | Not available in provided document/context | 5% – 10% (typical range) | Not calculable |
| Revenue | Not available in provided document/context | 0.5% – 1% (typical range) | Not calculable |
| Total Assets | Not available in provided document/context | 1% – 2% (typical range) | Not calculable |
| **Recommended Overall Materiality** | To be determined upon receipt of financial statements | Selected benchmark × applicable % | Not calculable |
| **Performance Materiality** | Typically 60% – 75% of overall materiality | Applied to reduce risk of aggregate uncorrected misstatements exceeding overall materiality | Not calculable |
| **Clearly Trivial Threshold** | Typically 3% – 5% of overall materiality | Misstatements below this threshold need not be accumulated | Not calculable |
| **Rationale for Selected Benchmark** | To be documented in audit working papers | Benchmark selection depends on entity type, profit stability, and primary user focus | To be confirmed |

---

# Risk Assessment

| Risk Area | Source Fact | Risk Description | Risk Level | Planned Audit Response |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Management Override / Fraud Risk | ISA 240 requires emphasis on susceptibility to material misstatement due to fraud during engagement team discussion [S2] | Management may override controls to manipulate financial results; inherent in all audits | High (presumed) | Journal entry testing; review of accounting estimates for bias; evaluation of unusual transactions; unpredictable audit procedures |
| Revenue Recognition / Cutoff | Not available in provided document/context | Risk of revenue being recorded in the incorrect period or recognised contrary to the applicable framework | To be assessed | Substantive testing of revenue transactions around period-end; analytical procedures; confirmation of terms |
| Inventory Valuation | Not available in provided document/context | Risk of inventory being overstated due to obsolescence, incorrect costing, or NRV issues | To be assessed | Attendance at physical inventory count; test of cost records; NRV assessment; review of slow-moving items |
| Trade Receivables Recoverability | Not available in provided document/context | Risk that receivables are overstated due to inadequate provisioning for doubtful debts | To be assessed | Aged debtor analysis; subsequent receipts testing; review of credit notes post period-end; direct confirmation |
| Related Party Transactions | ISA 315 (Revised 2019) and engagement team discussion requirements [S2] | Transactions with related parties may not be at arm's length or may be undisclosed | To be assessed | Obtain related party listing; review board minutes; confirm terms and disclosure adequacy |
| VAT / Corporate Tax Compliance | Not available in provided document/context | Risk of incorrect tax computations, under-provisioning, or non-compliance with tax legislation | To be assessed | Review tax computations; agree to returns filed; assess deferred tax; engage tax specialist where required |
| Going Concern | Not available in provided document/context | Risk that the entity may not be able to continue as a going concern for the foreseeable future | To be assessed | Review cash flow forecasts; assess covenant compliance; review post-balance sheet events; evaluate management's going concern assessment |
| Opening Balances (Initial Engagement) | Auditor must obtain sufficient appropriate evidence that opening balances do not contain material misstatements [S4] | Prior period misstatements may affect current period financial statements | To be assessed | Inspect predecessor auditor working papers; perform specific opening balance procedures; assess consistency of accounting policies [S4] |

---

# Audit Programs

**Inventory**
- Attend and observe physical inventory count at period-end; perform test counts and reconcile to inventory records
- Test cost of inventory including direct costs and overhead allocation; assess NRV against selling prices
- Review aged inventory listing; evaluate adequacy of obsolescence provisions
- Agree inventory quantities and values to the general ledger and financial statements

**Revenue**
- Perform analytical procedures on revenue by product/service line, period, and customer
- Test revenue transactions around period-end for correct cutoff; review credit notes issued post period-end
- Assess revenue recognition policies for compliance with the applicable financial reporting framework
- Vouch a sample of revenue transactions to supporting documentation (invoices, contracts, delivery records)

**Trade Receivables**
- Obtain and review aged receivables listing; assess recoverability of overdue balances
- Send direct confirmation requests to a sample of debtors; follow up on exceptions
- Test subsequent cash receipts for unconfirmed balances
- Evaluate adequacy of the bad debt provision; review credit notes and returns post period-end

**Related Parties**
- Obtain management's schedule of related parties and transactions; agree to supporting documentation
- Review board minutes, contracts, and loan agreements for undisclosed related party transactions
- Assess whether transactions are at arm's length and appropriately disclosed in the financial statements
- Confirm related party balances directly where material

**Tax / VAT**
- Review corporate tax computation; agree to the financial statements and filed returns
- Assess deferred tax asset/liability for completeness and accuracy; review temporary differences
- Review VAT reconciliations; agree output and input tax to underlying records and returns
- Engage tax specialist for complex or uncertain tax positions; assess adequacy of tax provisions

**Going Concern**
- Obtain and critically evaluate management's going concern assessment and supporting cash flow forecasts
- Review banking facilities, loan covenants, and compliance certificates
- Assess post-balance sheet events for indicators of financial difficulty
- Consider adequacy of going concern disclosures in the financial statements

**Financial Statement Close and Disclosures**
- Agree trial balance to financial statements; test journal entries including manual and top-side entries
- Review significant accounting estimates for reasonableness and consistency with prior periods
- Assess completeness and accuracy of disclosures against the applicable financial reporting framework
- Review financial statements for overall presentation, classification, and consistency

**Preliminary / Opening Procedures (Initial Engagement)**
- Communicate with predecessor auditor and review prior period audit report and working papers [S4]
- Perform opening balance procedures to confirm correct brought-forward balances and consistent accounting policies [S4]
- Evaluate any prior period audit opinion modifications and their effect on current period risk assessment [S4]

---

# Staffing Recommendations

| Role | Responsibility | Estimated Effort / Intensity |
|---|---|---|
| Engagement Partner | Overall responsibility for audit quality; review and approval of audit strategy and plan; sign-off on audit opinion; communication with those charged with governance [S1] | High review intensity; involvement at planning, key risk areas, and completion stages |
| Audit Manager | Day-to-day management of the engagement; supervision of audit team; review of working papers; liaison with client management [S2] | Continuous involvement throughout the engagement |
| Senior Auditor | Lead execution of risk-based audit procedures across significant account areas; preparation of key working papers; coaching of assistants | Highest time allocation; focused on high-risk areas (revenue, receivables, inventory) |
| Assistant Auditor(s) | Execution of substantive and compliance procedures under supervision; documentation of audit evidence | Allocated to lower-risk, higher-volume testing areas |
| Tax / Compliance Specialist | Review of corporate tax computations, deferred tax, VAT reconciliations, and uncertain tax positions | Engaged at interim and final stages; effort proportionate to tax complexity |
| IT Audit Specialist | Assessment of IT general controls and automated controls relevant to financial reporting (if applicable) | Engaged where significant reliance is placed on IT systems |
| Review Intensity | Proportionate to risk; high-risk areas (fraud, going concern, related parties) to receive engagement partner and manager review; lower-risk areas reviewed at manager level [S1] | Risk-stratified review approach |
| Estimated Effort by Risk Area | High: revenue, fraud/management override, going concern; Medium: receivables, inventory, tax; Lower: disclosures, opening balances (recurring engagement) | To be quantified in audit budget once financial statements and risk assessment are finalised |

citations.text: (Ref: Para. A5)   Preliminary Engagement Activities   6. The auditor shall undertake the following activities at the beginning of the current audit engagement:   (a) Performing procedures required by ISA 220 (Revised) regarding the acceptance and continuance of the client relationship and the specific audit engagement;1   (b) Evaluating compliance with relevant ethical requirements, including those related to independence, in accordance with ISA 220 (Revised),2 and   (c) Establishing an understanding of the terms of the engagement, as required by ISA 210.3 (Ref: Para. A6–A8)   Planning Activities 7. The auditor shall establish an overall audit strategy that sets the scope, timing and direction of the audit, and that guides the   development of the audit plan.   1 ISA 220 (Revised), Quality Management for an Audit of Financial Statements, paragraphs 22–24 2 ISA 220 (Revised), paragraphs 16–21 3 ISA 210, Agreeing the Terms of Audit Engagements, paragraphs 9–13PLANNING AN AUDIT OF FINANCI

The auditor may decide to discuss elements of planning with the entity’s management to help the auditor manage and achieve quality at the engagement level (for example, to coordinate some of the planned audit procedures with the work of the entity’s personnel). Although these discussions often occur, the overall audit strategy and the audit plan remain the auditor’s responsibility. When discussing matters included in the overall audit strategy or audit plan, care is required in order not to compromise the effectiveness of the audit. For example, discussing the nature and timing of detailed audit procedures with management may compromise the effectiveness of the audit by making the audit procedures too predictable.   Involvement of Key Engagement Team Members (Ref: Para. 5)   A5. The involvement of the engagement partner and other key members of the engagement team in planning the audit draws on their experience and insight, thereby enhancing the effectiveness and efficiency of the plan

A25. In some circumstances, those charged with governance may wish to be made aware of the details of other deficiencies in internal control the auditor has communicated to management, or be briefly informed of the nature of the other deficiencies. Alternatively, the auditor may consider it appropriate to inform those charged with governance of the communication of the other deficiencies to management. In either case, the auditor may report orally or in writing to those charged with governance as appropriate.   A26. ISA 260 (Revised) establishes relevant considerations regarding communication with those charged with governance when all of them are involved in managing the entity.11   Considerations Specific to Public Sector Entities (Ref: Para. 9–10)   A27. Public sector auditors may have additional responsibilities to communicate deficiencies in internal control that the auditor has identified during the audit, in ways, at a level of detail and to parties not envisaged in this ISA. Fo

Initial Audit Engagements   4.5.1. If the engagement is an initial audit and there has been a change in auditor, the auditor shall communicate with the predecessor auditor, in compliance with relevant ethical requirements.   4.5.2. The auditor shall read the most recent financial statements, if any, and the auditor’s report thereon, if any, for information relevant to opening balances, including disclosures.THE INTERNATIONAL STANDARD ON AUDITING FOR AUDITS OF FINANCIAL STATEMENTS OF LESS COMPLEX ENTITIES   THE ISA FOR LCE 32   4.5.3. If the prior period’s financial statements were audited by a predecessor auditor and there was a modification to the opinion, the auditor shall evaluate the effect of the matter giving rise to the modification in assessing the risks of material misstatement in the current period’s financial statements.9   4.5.4. The auditor shall obtain sufficient appropriate audit evidence10 about whether the opening balances contain misstatements that materially affect t
